pi.dev domain graciously donated by


exe.dev

> New issues and PRs from new contributors are auto-closed by default. Maintainers review auto-closed issues daily. See [CONTRIBUTING.md](../../CONTRIBUTING.md). --- Pi is a minimal terminal coding harness. Adapt pi to your workflows, not the other way around, without having to fork and modify pi internals. Extend it with TypeScript [Extensions](#extensions), [Skills](#skills), [Prompt Templates](#prompt-templates), and [Themes](#themes). Put your extensions, skills, prompt templates, and themes in [Pi Packages](#pi-packages) and share them with others via npm or git. Pi ships with powerful defaults but skips features like sub agents and plan mode. Instead, you can ask pi to build what you want or install a third party pi package that matches your workflow. Pi runs in four modes: interactive, print or JSON, RPC for process integration, and an SDK for embedding in your own apps. See [openclaw/openclaw](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw) for a real-world SDK integration. ## Share your OSS coding agent sessions If you use pi for open source work, please share your coding agent sessions. Public OSS session data helps improve models, prompts, tools, and evaluations using real development workflows. For the full explanation, see [this post on X](https://x.com/badlogicgames/status/2037811643774652911). To publish sessions, use [`badlogic/pi-share-hf`](https://github.com/badlogic/pi-share-hf). Read its README.md for setup instructions. All you need is a Hugging Face account, the Hugging Face CLI, and `pi-share-hf`. You can also watch [this video](https://x.com/badlogicgames/status/2041151967695634619), where I show how I publish my `pi-mono` sessions. I regularly publish my own `pi-mono` work sessions here: - [badlogicgames/pi-mono on Hugging Face](https://huggingface.co/datasets/badlogicgames/pi-mono) ## Table of Contents - [Quick Start](#quick-start) - [Providers & Models](#providers--models) - [Interactive Mode](#interactive-mode) - [Editor](#editor) - [Commands](#commands) - [Keyboard Shortcuts](#keyboard-shortcuts) - [Message Queue](#message-queue) - [Sessions](#sessions) - [Branching](#branching) - [Compaction](#compaction) - [Settings](#settings) - [Context Files](#context-files) - [Customization](#customization) - [Prompt Templates](#prompt-templates) - [Skills](#skills) - [Extensions](#extensions) - [Themes](#themes) - [Pi Packages](#pi-packages) - [Programmatic Usage](#programmatic-usage) - [Philosophy](#philosophy) - [CLI Reference](#cli-reference) --- ## Quick Start ```bash npm install -g @mariozechner/pi-coding-agent ``` Authenticate with an API key: ```bash export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-... pi ``` Or use your existing subscription: ```bash pi /login # Then select provider ``` Then just talk to pi. By default, pi gives the model four tools: `read`, `write`, `edit`, and `bash`. The model uses these to fulfill your requests. Add capabilities via [skills](#skills), [prompt templates](#prompt-templates), [extensions](#extensions), or [pi packages](#pi-packages). **Platform notes:** [Windows](docs/windows.md) | [Termux (Android)](docs/termux.md) | [tmux](docs/tmux.md) | [Terminal setup](docs/terminal-setup.md) | [Shell aliases](docs/shell-aliases.md) --- ## Providers & Models For each built-in provider, pi maintains a list of tool-capable models, updated with every release. Authenticate via subscription (`/login`) or API key, then select any model from that provider via `/model` (or Ctrl+L). **Subscriptions:** - Anthropic Claude Pro/Max - OpenAI ChatGPT Plus/Pro (Codex) - GitHub Copilot - Google Gemini CLI - Google Antigravity **API keys:** - Anthropic - OpenAI - Azure OpenAI - DeepSeek - Google Gemini - Google Vertex - Amazon Bedrock - Mistral - Groq - Cerebras - Cloudflare Workers AI - xAI - OpenRouter - Vercel AI Gateway - ZAI - OpenCode Zen - OpenCode Go - Hugging Face - Fireworks - Kimi For Coding - MiniMax See [docs/providers.md](docs/providers.md) for detailed setup instructions. **Custom providers & models:** Add providers via `~/.pi/agent/models.json` if they speak a supported API (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google). For custom APIs or OAuth, use extensions. See [docs/models.md](docs/models.md) and [docs/custom-provider.md](docs/custom-provider.md). --- ## Interactive Mode

The interface from top to bottom: - **Startup header** - Shows shortcuts (`/hotkeys` for all), loaded AGENTS.md files, prompt templates, skills, and extensions - **Messages** - Your messages, assistant responses, tool calls and results, notifications, errors, and extension UI - **Editor** - Where you type; border color indicates thinking level - **Footer** - Working directory, session name, total token/cache usage, cost, context usage, current model The editor can be temporarily replaced by other UI, like built-in `/settings` or custom UI from extensions (e.g., a Q&A tool that lets the user answer model questions in a structured format). [Extensions](#extensions) can also replace the editor, add widgets above/below it, a status line, custom footer, or overlays. ### Editor | Feature | How | |---------|-----| | File reference | Type `@` to fuzzy-search project files | | Path completion | Tab to complete paths | | Multi-line | Shift+Enter (or Ctrl+Enter on Windows Terminal) | | Images | Ctrl+V to paste (Alt+V on Windows), or drag onto terminal | | Bash commands | `!command` runs and sends output to LLM, `!!command` runs without sending | Standard editing keybindings for delete word, undo, etc. See [docs/keybindings.md](docs/keybindings.md). ### Commands Type `/` in the editor to trigger commands. [Extensions](#extensions) can register custom commands, [skills](#skills) are available as `/skill:name`, and [prompt templates](#prompt-templates) expand via `/templatename`. | Command | Description | |---------|-------------| | `/login`, `/logout` | OAuth authentication | | `/model` | Switch models | | `/scoped-models` | Enable/disable models for Ctrl+P cycling | | `/settings` | Thinking level, theme, message delivery, transport | | `/resume` | Pick from previous sessions | | `/new` | Start a new session | | `/name ` | Set session display name | | `/session` | Show session info (file, ID, messages, tokens, cost) | | `/tree` | Jump to any point in the session and continue from there | | `/fork` | Create a new session from a previous user message | | `/clone` | Duplicate the current active branch into a new session | | `/compact [prompt]` | Manually compact context, optional custom instructions | | `/copy` | Copy last assistant message to clipboard | | `/export [file]` | Export session to HTML file | | `/share` | Upload as private GitHub gist with shareable HTML link | | `/reload` | Reload keybindings, extensions, skills, prompts, and context files (themes hot-reload automatically) | | `/hotkeys` | Show all keyboard shortcuts | | `/changelog` | Display version history | | `/quit` | Quit pi | ### Keyboard Shortcuts See `/hotkeys` for the full list. Customize via `~/.pi/agent/keybindings.json`. See [docs/keybindings.md](docs/keybindings.md). **Commonly used:** | Key | Action | |-----|--------| | Ctrl+C | Clear editor | | Ctrl+C twice | Quit | | Escape | Cancel/abort | | Escape twice | Open `/tree` | | Ctrl+L | Open model selector | | Ctrl+P / Shift+Ctrl+P | Cycle scoped models forward/backward | | Shift+Tab | Cycle thinking level | | Ctrl+O | Collapse/expand tool output | | Ctrl+T | Collapse/expand thinking blocks | ### Message Queue Submit messages while the agent is working: - **Enter** queues a *steering* message, delivered after the current assistant turn finishes executing its tool calls - **Alt+Enter** queues a *follow-up* message, delivered only after the agent finishes all work - **Escape** aborts and restores queued messages to editor - **Alt+Up** retrieves queued messages back to editor On Windows Terminal, `Alt+Enter` is fullscreen by default. Remap it in [docs/terminal-setup.md](docs/terminal-setup.md) so pi can receive the follow-up shortcut. Configure delivery in [settings](docs/settings.md): `steeringMode` and `followUpMode` can be `"one-at-a-time"` (default, waits for response) or `"all"` (delivers all queued at once). `transport` selects provider transport preference (`"sse"`, `"websocket"`, or `"auto"`) for providers that support multiple transports. --- ## Sessions Sessions are stored as JSONL files with a tree structure. Each entry has an `id` and `parentId`, enabling in-place branching without creating new files. See [docs/session-format.md](docs/session-format.md) for file format. ### Management Sessions auto-save to `~/.pi/agent/sessions/` organized by working directory. ```bash pi -c # Continue most recent session pi -r # Browse and select from past sessions pi --no-session # Ephemeral mode (don't save) pi --session # Use specific session file or ID pi --fork # Fork specific session file or ID into a new session ``` Use `/session` in interactive mode to see the current session ID before reusing it with `--session ` or `--fork `. ### Branching **`/tree`** - Navigate the session tree in-place. Select any previous point, continue from there, and switch between branches. All history preserved in a single file.

- Search by typing, fold/unfold and jump between branches with Ctrl+←/Ctrl+→ or Alt+←/Alt+→, page with ←/→ - Filter modes (Ctrl+O): default → no-tools → user-only → labeled-only → all - Press Shift+L to label entries as bookmarks and Shift+T to toggle label timestamps **`/fork`** - Create a new session file from a previous user message on the active branch. Opens a selector, copies the active path up to that point, and places the selected prompt in the editor for modification. **`/clone`** - Duplicate the current active branch into a new session file at the current position. The new session keeps the full active-path history and opens with an empty editor. **`--fork `** - Fork an existing session file or partial session UUID directly from the CLI. This copies the full source session into a new session file in the current project. ### Compaction Long sessions can exhaust context windows. Compaction summarizes older messages while keeping recent ones. **Manual:** `/compact` or `/compact ` **Automatic:** Enabled by default. Triggers on context overflow (recovers and retries) or when approaching the limit (proactive). Configure via `/settings` or `settings.json`. Compaction is lossy. The full history remains in the JSONL file; use `/tree` to revisit. Customize compaction behavior via [extensions](#extensions). See [docs/compaction.md](docs/compaction.md) for internals. --- ## Settings Use `/settings` to modify common options, or edit JSON files directly: | Location | Scope | |----------|-------| | `~/.pi/agent/settings.json` | Global (all projects) | | `.pi/settings.json` | Project (overrides global) | See [docs/settings.md](docs/settings.md) for all options. To opt out of anonymous install/update telemetry tied to changelog detection, set `enableInstallTelemetry` to `false` in `settings.json`, or set `PI_TELEMETRY=0`. --- ## Context Files Pi loads `AGENTS.md` (or `CLAUDE.md`) at startup from: - `~/.pi/agent/AGENTS.md` (global) - Parent directories (walking up from cwd) - Current directory Use for project instructions, conventions, common commands. All matching files are concatenated. Disable context file loading with `--no-context-files` (or `-nc`). ### System Prompt Replace the default system prompt with `.pi/SYSTEM.md` (project) or `~/.pi/agent/SYSTEM.md` (global). Append without replacing via `APPEND_SYSTEM.md`. --- ## Customization ### Prompt Templates Reusable prompts as Markdown files. Type `/name` to expand. ```markdown Review this code for bugs, security issues, and performance problems. Focus on: {{focus}} ``` Place in `~/.pi/agent/prompts/`, `.pi/prompts/`, or a [pi package](#pi-packages) to share with others. See [docs/prompt-templates.md](docs/prompt-templates.md). ### Skills On-demand capability packages following the [Agent Skills standard](https://agentskills.io). Invoke via `/skill:name` or let the agent load them automatically. ```markdown # My Skill Use this skill when the user asks about X. ## Steps 1. Do this 2. Then that ``` Place in `~/.pi/agent/skills/`, `~/.agents/skills/`, `.pi/skills/`, or `.agents/skills/` (from `cwd` up through parent directories) or a [pi package](#pi-packages) to share with others. See [docs/skills.md](docs/skills.md). ### Extensions

TypeScript modules that extend pi with custom tools, commands, keyboard shortcuts, event handlers, and UI components. ```typescript export default function (pi: ExtensionAPI) { pi.registerTool({ name: "deploy", ... }); pi.registerCommand("stats", { ... }); pi.on("tool_call", async (event, ctx) => { ... }); } ``` The default export can also be `async`. pi waits for async extension factories before startup continues, which is useful for one-time initialization such as fetching remote model lists before calling `pi.registerProvider()`. **What's possible:** - Custom tools (or replace built-in tools entirely) - Sub-agents and plan mode - Custom compaction and summarization - Permission gates and path protection - Custom editors and UI components - Status lines, headers, footers - Git checkpointing and auto-commit - SSH and sandbox execution - MCP server integration - Make pi look like Claude Code - Games while waiting (yes, Doom runs) - ...anything you can dream up Place in `~/.pi/agent/extensions/`, `.pi/extensions/`, or a [pi package](#pi-packages) to share with others. See [docs/extensions.md](docs/extensions.md) and [examples/extensions/](examples/extensions/). ### Themes Built-in: `dark`, `light`. Themes hot-reload: modify the active theme file and pi immediately applies changes. Place in `~/.pi/agent/themes/`, `.pi/themes/`, or a [pi package](#pi-packages) to share with others. See [docs/themes.md](docs/themes.md). ### Pi Packages Bundle and share extensions, skills, prompts, and themes via npm or git. Find packages on [npmjs.com](https://www.npmjs.com/search?q=keywords%3Api-package) or [Discord](https://discord.com/channels/1456806362351669492/1457744485428629628). > **Security:** Pi packages run with full system access. Extensions execute arbitrary code, and skills can instruct the model to perform any action including running executables. Review source code before installing third-party packages. ```bash pi install npm:@foo/pi-tools pi install npm:@foo/pi-tools@1.2.3 # pinned version pi install git:github.com/user/repo pi install git:github.com/user/repo@v1 # tag or commit pi install git:git@github.com:user/repo pi install git:git@github.com:user/repo@v1 # tag or commit pi install https://github.com/user/repo pi install https://github.com/user/repo@v1 # tag or commit pi install ssh://git@github.com/user/repo pi install ssh://git@github.com/user/repo@v1 # tag or commit pi remove npm:@foo/pi-tools pi uninstall npm:@foo/pi-tools # alias for remove pi list pi update # update pi and packages (skips pinned packages) pi update --extensions # update packages only pi update --self # update pi only pi update --self --force # reinstall pi even if current pi update npm:@foo/pi-tools # update one package pi config # enable/disable extensions, skills, prompts, themes ``` Packages install to `~/.pi/agent/git/` (git) or global npm. Use `-l` for project-local installs (`.pi/git/`, `.pi/npm/`). Git packages install dependencies with `npm install --omit=dev` by default, so runtime deps must be listed under `dependencies`; when `npmCommand` is configured, git packages use plain `install` for compatibility with wrappers. If you use a Node version manager and want package installs to reuse a stable npm context, set `npmCommand` in `settings.json`, for example `["mise", "exec", "node@20", "--", "npm"]`. Create a package by adding a `pi` key to `package.json`: ```json { "name": "my-pi-package", "keywords": ["pi-package"], "pi": { "extensions": ["./extensions"], "skills": ["./skills"], "prompts": ["./prompts"], "themes": ["./themes"] } } ``` Without a `pi` manifest, pi auto-discovers from conventional directories (`extensions/`, `skills/`, `prompts/`, `themes/`). See [docs/packages.md](docs/packages.md). --- ## Programmatic Usage ### SDK ```typescript import { AuthStorage, createAgentSession, ModelRegistry, SessionManager } from "@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent"; const authStorage = AuthStorage.create(); const modelRegistry = ModelRegistry.create(authStorage); const { session } = await createAgentSession({ sessionManager: SessionManager.inMemory(), authStorage, modelRegistry, }); await session.prompt("What files are in the current directory?"); ``` For advanced multi-session runtime replacement, use `createAgentSessionRuntime()` and `AgentSessionRuntime`. See [docs/sdk.md](docs/sdk.md) and [examples/sdk/](examples/sdk/). ### RPC Mode For non-Node.js integrations, use RPC mode over stdin/stdout: ```bash pi --mode rpc ``` RPC mode uses strict LF-delimited JSONL framing. Clients must split records on `\n` only. Do not use generic line readers like Node `readline`, which also split on Unicode separators inside JSON payloads. See [docs/rpc.md](docs/rpc.md) for the protocol. --- ## Philosophy Pi is aggressively extensible so it doesn't have to dictate your workflow. Features that other tools bake in can be built with [extensions](#extensions), [skills](#skills), or installed from third-party [pi packages](#pi-packages). This keeps the core minimal while letting you shape pi to fit how you work. **No MCP.** Build CLI tools with READMEs (see [Skills](#skills)), or build an extension that adds MCP support. [Why?](https://mariozechner.at/posts/2025-11-02-what-if-you-dont-need-mcp/) **No sub-agents.** There's many ways to do this. Spawn pi instances via tmux, or build your own with [extensions](#extensions), or install a package that does it your way. **No permission popups.** Run in a container, or build your own confirmation flow with [extensions](#extensions) inline with your environment and security requirements. **No plan mode.** Write plans to files, or build it with [extensions](#extensions), or install a package. **No built-in to-dos.** They confuse models. Use a TODO.md file, or build your own with [extensions](#extensions). **No background bash.** Use tmux. Full observability, direct interaction. Read the [blog post](https://mariozechner.at/posts/2025-11-30-pi-coding-agent/) for the full rationale. --- ## CLI Reference ```bash pi [options] [@files...] [messages...] ``` ### Package Commands ```bash pi install [-l] # Install package, -l for project-local pi remove [-l] # Remove package pi uninstall [-l] # Alias for remove pi update [source|self|pi] # Update pi and packages (skips pinned packages) pi update --extensions # Update packages only pi update --self # Update pi only pi update --self --force # Reinstall pi even if current pi update --extension # Update one package pi list # List installed packages pi config # Enable/disable package resources ``` ### Modes | Flag | Description | |------|-------------| | (default) | Interactive mode | | `-p`, `--print` | Print response and exit | | `--mode json` | Output all events as JSON lines (see [docs/json.md](docs/json.md)) | | `--mode rpc` | RPC mode for process integration (see [docs/rpc.md](docs/rpc.md)) | | `--export [out]` | Export session to HTML | In print mode, pi also reads piped stdin and merges it into the initial prompt: ```bash cat README.md | pi -p "Summarize this text" ``` ### Model Options | Option | Description | |--------|-------------| | `--provider ` | Provider (anthropic, openai, google, etc.) | | `--model ` | Model pattern or ID (supports `provider/id` and optional `:`) | | `--api-key ` | API key (overrides env vars) | | `--thinking ` | `off`, `minimal`, `low`, `medium`, `high`, `xhigh` | | `--models ` | Comma-separated patterns for Ctrl+P cycling | | `--list-models [search]` | List available models | ### Session Options | Option | Description | |--------|-------------| | `-c`, `--continue` | Continue most recent session | | `-r`, `--resume` | Browse and select session | | `--session ` | Use specific session file or partial UUID | | `--fork ` | Fork specific session file or partial UUID into a new session | | `--session-dir ` | Custom session storage directory | | `--no-session` | Ephemeral mode (don't save) | ### Tool Options | Option | Description | |--------|-------------| | `--tools `, `-t ` | Allowlist specific tool names across built-in, extension, and custom tools | | `--no-builtin-tools`, `-nbt` | Disable built-in tools by default but keep extension/custom tools enabled | | `--no-tools`, `-nt` | Disable all tools by default | Available built-in tools: `read`, `bash`, `edit`, `write`, `grep`, `find`, `ls` ### Resource Options | Option | Description | |--------|-------------| | `-e`, `--extension ` | Load extension from path, npm, or git (repeatable) | | `--no-extensions` | Disable extension discovery | | `--skill ` | Load skill (repeatable) | | `--no-skills` | Disable skill discovery | | `--prompt-template ` | Load prompt template (repeatable) | | `--no-prompt-templates` | Disable prompt template discovery | | `--theme ` | Load theme (repeatable) | | `--no-themes` | Disable theme discovery | | `--no-context-files`, `-nc` | Disable AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md context file discovery | Combine `--no-*` with explicit flags to load exactly what you need, ignoring settings.json (e.g., `--no-extensions -e ./my-ext.ts`). ### Other Options | Option | Description | |--------|-------------| | `--system-prompt ` | Replace default prompt (context files and skills still appended) | | `--append-system-prompt ` | Append to system prompt | | `--verbose` | Force verbose startup | | `-h`, `--help` | Show help | | `-v`, `--version` | Show version | ### File Arguments Prefix files with `@` to include in the message: ```bash pi @prompt.md "Answer this" pi -p @screenshot.png "What's in this image?" pi @code.ts @test.ts "Review these files" ``` ### Examples ```bash # Interactive with initial prompt pi "List all .ts files in src/" # Non-interactive pi -p "Summarize this codebase" # Non-interactive with piped stdin cat README.md | pi -p "Summarize this text" # Different model pi --provider openai --model gpt-4o "Help me refactor" # Model with provider prefix (no --provider needed) pi --model openai/gpt-4o "Help me refactor" # Model with thinking level shorthand pi --model sonnet:high "Solve this complex problem" # Limit model cycling pi --models "claude-*,gpt-4o" # Read-only mode pi --tools read,grep,find,ls -p "Review the code" # High thinking level pi --thinking high "Solve this complex problem" ``` ### Environment Variables | Variable | Description | |----------|-------------| | `PI_CODING_AGENT_DIR` | Override config directory (default: `~/.pi/agent`) | | `PI_PACKAGE_DIR` | Override package directory (useful for Nix/Guix where store paths tokenize poorly) | | `PI_SKIP_VERSION_CHECK` | Skip version check at startup | | `PI_TELEMETRY` | Override install telemetry. Use `1`/`true`/`yes` to enable or `0`/`false`/`no` to disable | | `PI_CACHE_RETENTION` | Set to `long` for extended prompt cache (Anthropic: 1h, OpenAI: 24h) | | `VISUAL`, `EDITOR` | External editor for Ctrl+G | --- ## Contributing & Development See [CONTRIBUTING.md](../../CONTRIBUTING.md) for guidelines and [docs/development.md](docs/development.md) for setup, forking, and debugging. --- ## License MIT ## See Also - [@mariozechner/pi-ai](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@mariozechner/pi-ai): Core LLM toolkit - [@mariozechner/pi-agent](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@mariozechner/pi-agent): Agent framework - [@mariozechner/pi-tui](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@mariozechner/pi-tui): Terminal UI components

content.text:
**Pi** is a minimal, highly extensible terminal coding harness designed to adapt to your specific workflow rather than forcing a specific way of working on you. 

Here are the key aspects of the Pi harness:

### 1. Core Philosophy
Pi ships with powerful defaults but intentionally skips complex built-in features like sub-agents, plan modes, or MCP (Model Context Protocol). Instead, it relies on an aggressive extensibility model so you can build or install exactly the features you want. By default, it gives the model access to fundamental tools: `read`, `write`, `edit`, and `bash`.

### 2. Operating Modes
Pi can run in four different modes:
*   **Interactive Mode:** A rich terminal UI featuring a multi-line editor, slash commands (like `/settings`, `/model`), keyboard shortcuts, path completion, file referencing (using `@`), and tree-based session history navigation.
*   **Print / JSON Mode:** Standard CLI execution for non-interactive use or piping inputs/outputs.
*   **RPC Mode:** Uses strict LF-delimited JSONL for programmatic integration into other non-Node.js processes.
*   **SDK Mode:** Allows you to embed Pi programmatically into your own TypeScript/Node.js apps.

### 3. Deep Customization & Extensibility
You can shape Pi to your needs using several mechanisms, which can be bundled and shared as **Pi Packages** via npm or Git:
*   **Extensions (TypeScript):** Create custom tools, UI components, custom commands, or event handlers. You can even use extensions to add things like MCP support or sub-agents.
*   **Skills:** On-demand capabilities using Markdown files that follow the Agent Skills standard (e.g., teaching the agent how to do a specific task).
*   **Prompt Templates:** Reusable Markdown snippets you can expand in the editor using `/templatename`.
*   **Themes:** Customizable UI colors that hot-reload automatically.

### 4. Advanced Session Management
Pi tracks your conversation in a unique tree structure saved as a JSONL file. 
*   **`/tree`:** Navigate the session tree in-place, allowing you to jump back to any previous point in the conversation and branch off from there without losing history.
*   **`/fork` & `/clone`:** Easily split off different branches of thought into their own separate session files.
*   **Compaction:** Automatically summarizes older messages when you approach the context window limit while keeping recent history intact.

### 5. Context Awareness
Upon startup, Pi automatically searches your current and parent directories for `AGENTS.md` or `CLAUDE.md` files to load project-specific instructions, coding conventions, and common commands into its context.

### 6. Broad Model Support
Pi supports a massive variety of models and providers either via subscription (`/login`) or API keys. Supported providers include Anthropic, OpenAI, Google Gemini, GitHub Copilot, DeepSeek, Mistral, Groq, xAI, OpenRouter, and many more.

You can trigger commands in the interactive UI using `/`, quickly switch models using `Ctrl+L`, or install third-party capabilities using the `pi install` CLI command.

# Identity: Systems Engineer
- **Core Principles**: Builds distributed applications across cloud and edge environments, maximizing explicit control, minimizing dependency footprints, and prioritizing automation over manual processes.
- **Operational Constraints**: Always use `git worktree` and `subagent-driven-development` workflows; default to Caveman mode; always create a plan before execution.
- **Output Preferences**: Direct, concise, technical; minimize boilerplate and fluff; maximize code quality over quantity.
- **Behavioral Flags**: Challenge user assumptions (do not pander); debug across application, OS, and hardware layers; verify assumptions step-by-step using raw logs and build outputs.

# Environment: Codeweaver Workstation
- **Directory**: `~/.agents` (global context — synced via git)
- **Aliases Available**:
  - Git: `gs`, `ga`, `gc`, `gp`, `gl`, `gd`, `gb`, `gco`, `glog`
  - Nav: `..`, `...`
  - Config: `bashrc-edit`, `dotfiles-edit`, `i3-edit`
  - Opencode: `opencode-cleanup`
  - (TODO in bashrc): `cdntv`, `cdapi`, `cddash`, `cdplayer`, `cdui`, `dotnet-build`, `dotnet-run`, `dotnet-test`

**Active Technologies**:
- Core: Node.js (OpenCode), bash, git worktrees
- Agents: Markdown system prompts
- Skills: TypeScript/Python MCPs

**Known Constraints**:
- Must avoid auto-injecting vector memory to save tokens. Use file-based read.

## Key Paths & Repositories
- `/data/dev/work/ntv/player-ui`
- `/data/dev/work/ntv/api-v1`
- `/data/dev/work/ntv/dashboard-v1`
- `~/.config/opencode/` — OpenCode config repo (separate git repo, synced via mcp-broker)
- `~/.agents/` — Global agent context repo (this repo, synced via git)

## Notes & Knowledge Base
- **Obsidian Vault**: `~/Dropbox/Obsidian/` — synced via Syncthing across all devices
- **Structure**: PARA method (Projects, Areas, Resources, Archives, System)
- **Agent Access**: Write notes directly via file system tools; vault `AGENTS.md` at `~/Dropbox/Obsidian/AGENTS.md` documents naming conventions, folder structure, and templates

## Device Topology & Sync
- **Work PC (Local)**: Linux, i3wm (`~/dotfiles/wm/i3`), AstroNvim (`~/Projects/personal/config-astronvim`).
- **Windows Laptop (Remote)**: WSL2 environment.
- **Tablet/Phone (Android)**: Mobile access.
- **Sync Mechanism**: Tailscale mesh network for SSH/handoff. Syncthing for file/Obsidian sync across all nodes.
- **AI Strategy (Goal)**: Centralized controller on Work PC dispatches agents to mobile/laptop via Tailscale SSH.

# Superpowers Workflow

## Phases

```
1. Brainstorming — understand and design before any code
2. Writing Plans — create the roadmap before execution
3. Using Git Worktrees — set up isolated workspace
4. Subagent-Driven Development — execute per-task with two-stage review
5. Finishing a Development Branch — complete and integrate
```

---

## Phase 1: Brainstorming (HARD-GATE)

**Non-optional for ALL creative work.**

Creative work = ideas, new setups, architectures, implementations, config maintenance, modifying behavior.

**Must NOT:**
- Write any code
- Scaffold any project
- Take any implementation action

**Until:**
1. Explored project context
2. Asked clarifying questions (one at a time)
3. Proposed 2-3 approaches with trade-offs
4. Presented design and received user approval
5. Written design doc to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md`

---

## Phase 2: Writing Plans

After design approved:
1. Create detailed implementation plan with:
   - Exact file paths
   - Complete code for each step
   - Verification commands
   - 2-5 minute tasks
2. Plan finalized before any execution
3. **Execution recommendation:** After plan finalized, recommend approach:
   - **Subagent-Driven:** complex tasks, multi-file, safety-critical
   - **Inline:** simple, single-file, trivial config

---

## Phase 3: Using Git Worktrees

Before ANY implementation:
1. Create isolated workspace on new branch
2. Run project setup
3. Verify clean test baseline

**Never work on main/master without explicit user consent.**

---

## Phase 4: Subagent-Driven Development

### Per-Task Flow
```
1. Read plan → extract task → provide exact context needed
2. Dispatch coder subagent
3. Coder may ask questions → answer BEFORE work begins
4. Coder completes, self-reviews, commits
5. Dispatch QA — spec compliance review
   - FAIL → coder fixes → re-review
6. Dispatch QA — code quality review
   - FAIL → coder fixes → re-review
7. Mark task complete
8. Next task
```

### Task Routing
| Task Type              | Route To | Notes                       |
| ---------------------- | -------- | --------------------------- |
| Implementation         | @coder   | Subagent-driven-development |
| Spec / Quality review  | @qa      | Two-stage, spec first       |

---

## Phase 5: Finishing a Development Branch

After ALL tasks complete:
1. Dispatch final code-reviewer for entire implementation
2. Options: merge, PR, keep, or discard
3. Clean up worktree

---

## Docs Structure
```
docs/superpowers/
├── specs/   YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md
└── plans/   YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>.md
```

content.text:
Based on the files and project context provided, the `.agents` setup is a **highly structured, opinionated, and sophisticated agent orchestration framework**. It moves away from relying on a single, monolithic AI assistant prompt and instead behaves like an entire virtual engineering team.

Here is an analysis of its core strengths and setup:

### 1. Modular Context Vault
Instead of cramming everything into one massive system prompt, the setup splits context into focused, composable markdown files (`identity.md`, `environment.md`, `long-term.md`). This allows for dynamic context loading—the agent only reads what it needs, keeping token usage low and preventing the model from getting confused by irrelevant instructions.

### 2. Strict, Multi-Phase Workflow ("Superpowers")
The `superpowers.md` workflow strictly regulates how work gets done using a 5-phase framework:
1. **Brainstorming (Hard-Gate):** Explicitly forbids the agent from writing code until context is explored and a design doc is approved by the user.
2. **Planning:** Forces the generation of a roadmap with exact file paths and verification commands.
3. **Worktrees:** Enforces the use of `git worktree` to isolate changes and protect the `main` branch.
4. **Subagent-Driven Development:** Tasks are broken down and routed to specific personas.
5. **Finishing:** Final review and cleanup.

This structure prevents the AI from "going rogue," writing massive amounts of hallucinated code, or executing destructive actions without thinking it through first.

### 3. Role-Based Delegation
The `roles/` directory defines distinct personas (`controller.md`, `coder.md`, `qa.md`). 
* The **Controller** acts as a tech lead, talking to you and orchestrating the plan.
* The **Coder** acts as the implementer.
* The **QA** enforces spec compliance and code quality via a two-stage review process.
By separating these concerns, it combats the LLM tendency to grade its own homework too leniently.

### 4. Hyper-Optimized Communication
The identity explicitly demands a "direct, concise, technical" tone with "minimal boilerplate and fluff." It strongly favors a **"Caveman mode"** to drastically cut token usage (by up to ~75%) while preserving technical accuracy, which is brilliant for long-running, iterative CLI sessions. It is also instructed to challenge user assumptions rather than blindly pandering to bad ideas.

### 5. Deep Environment Integration
The environment isn't generic; it's tightly bound to the `Codeweaver` workstation:
* **Knowledge Base:** Direct integration with an Obsidian Vault (`~/Dropbox/Obsidian/`) using the PARA method via the `obsidian` skill, acting as a persistent long-term memory system across sessions.
* **Domain Awareness:** Deep knowledge of the local NTV ecosystem (`api-v1`, `dashboard-v1`, `player-server`, `player-ui`), dotfiles, and system utilities.
* **Network Topology:** It understands that it operates on a Tailscale mesh network, paving the way for remote dispatch (e.g., controlling a remote Raspberry Pi or WSL environment).

### Summary
It is an impressively advanced local agent harness. By enforcing upfront design docs, isolating work in git worktrees, delegating to sub-roles, and enforcing a strict QA pipeline, it optimizes the LLM for **reliability and safety over immediate, unguided code generation**.

content.text:
Moving the `~/.agents` setup into your Obsidian vault is an interesting idea, but from a systems engineering perspective, **I recommend keeping them separate.** 

While unifying everything into Obsidian sounds clean on paper, the current separation of concerns between `git` (for agent configuration) and `Syncthing/Obsidian` (for human knowledge) is technically superior. Here is why:

### 1. Version Control vs. File Synchronization
* **`~/.agents` relies on Git:** Agent configurations, workflows, and skills are effectively "source code" for your AI. When you tweak a prompt, modify a workflow, or update a skill, you want to be able to diff those changes, revert if a prompt regression causes the AI to hallucinate, and branch to test new agent behaviors. 
* **Obsidian relies on Syncthing:** Syncthing is fantastic for immediate file replication across devices, but it lacks atomic commits, discrete branching, and easy point-in-time rollbacks. If an agent accidentally corrupts a critical skill file in Obsidian, restoring it via Syncthing conflicts is much harder than running `git restore`.

### 2. Separation of Concerns (Execution vs. Knowledge)
* **`~/.agents` is Executable System State:** It contains strict protocols, CLI skill manifests, TypeScript/Python MCP server definitions, and rigid machine-readable structures. 
* **Obsidian is Semantic Memory:** It holds human-readable design docs, meeting notes, work logs, and high-level architectural plans. 
* Mixing raw system configuration files into your personal knowledge base clutters your Obsidian search results with boilerplate prompt instructions and operational directives.

### 3. Path Stability & Symlinking Constraints
Many agent frameworks (like Pi, OpenCode, and Claude Code) rely heavily on predictable, stable paths to load plugins and context. Your current setup elegantly symlinks `~/.config/opencode/skills` to `~/.agents/roles`. 
* Moving this into `~/Dropbox/Obsidian/...` introduces the risk of file-sync locks, path spaces, or cloud-sync delays breaking local CLI tools if the agent tries to load a skill while Syncthing is actively updating the database.

### The Ideal Compromise: Bridge the Gap, Don't Merge
Rather than moving the files, you can treat `~/.agents` as the **operating system** and Obsidian as the **hard drive**:
1. **Keep `~/.agents` in Git:** Maintain it as your immutable, version-controlled repository for agent behavior, skills, and configuration.
2. **Let Agents Read/Write to Obsidian:** Use the existing `obsidian` skill to let the agents treat the vault as their long-term memory for storing output (like the `docs/superpowers/specs/` design docs and daily work logs).
3. **Index `.agents` in Obsidian (Optional):** If you just want the ability to read your agent prompts inside the Obsidian UI, you can symlink `~/.agents` into a dedicated folder in your vault (e.g., `~/Dropbox/Obsidian/System/Agents/`). This gives you read-access in your note-taking app without fundamentally changing how the agents operate at the OS level.

Yes, the current `~/.agents` setup is **highly compatible structurally** with the Pi harness, but there are some **philosophical and execution differences** you will need to account for. 

Pi actually seems designed with setups exactly like yours in mind, but it requires you to build the "glue" via extensions rather than relying on built-in magic. Here is a breakdown of how your setup maps to Pi:

### 1. Structural Compatibility: Native Support (Excellent)
* **Skills Directory:** Pi natively supports your skill architecture. According to the Pi README, it explicitly looks for skills in `~/.agents/skills/` and `.agents/skills/` out-of-the-box. Your `SKILL.md` format perfectly aligns with the Agent Skills standard that Pi uses.
* **Context Files (`AGENTS.md`):** Pi automatically discovers and loads `AGENTS.md` files from the current working directory and its parents. 
* **Global Context:** Pi allows global system prompts via `~/.pi/agent/SYSTEM.md` or `~/.pi/agent/AGENTS.md`. You could easily symlink your `~/.agents/AGENTS.md` to `~/.pi/agent/AGENTS.md`.

### 2. Workflow Compatibility: Sub-Agents (Needs Adaptation)
* **The `.agents` Setup:** Your `superpowers.md` workflow strictly dictates "Subagent-Driven Development" (routing tasks between `@coder`, `@qa`, and a central controller).
* **The Pi Harness:** Pi explicitly states in its philosophy: *"No sub-agents... build your own with extensions."* 
* **The Fix:** Out-of-the-box, Pi acts as a single continuous agent. To replicate your sub-agent workflow, you have two options:
  1. **Prompt-driven (Easy):** Manually tell Pi to switch personas (e.g., "Now act as the `@qa` role and review the code you just wrote using `~/.agents/roles/qa.md`").
  2. **Extension-driven (Advanced):** Write a custom Pi Extension (`.ts`) that intercepts tasks and programmatically spawns new background Pi sessions or branches using Pi's programmatic SDK/RPC mode to act as the sub-agents.

### 3. File Inclusion Syntax (Needs Adaptation)
* **The `.agents` Setup:** Your `AGENTS.md` uses Claude Code's syntax (`@.agents/context/identity.md`) to dynamically inject context files.
* **The Pi Harness:** Pi concatenates `AGENTS.md` files, but its `@` syntax is designed for interactive fuzzy-searching in the terminal UI editor, not necessarily for auto-expanding includes inside the markdown files themselves upon load.
* **The Fix:** You would likely need to write a tiny Pi Extension that hooks into the startup sequence to parse and flatten those `@` includes into the system prompt, or run a simple bash script to compile them into a single `.pi/SYSTEM.md` file.

### 4. MCP Broker Integration (Needs Extension)
* **The `.agents` Setup:** Relies heavily on an Opencode MCP Broker for custom tools.
* **The Pi Harness:** Pi explicitly says *"No MCP... build an extension that adds MCP support."*
* **The Fix:** You will need to write or install a Pi package/extension that connects Pi's `registerTool` API to your existing TypeScript MCP broker. Because Pi extensions are just TypeScript, mapping MCP tools to Pi tools is straightforward.

### Summary
Your `~/.agents` folder is a perfect candidate for a **Pi Package**. 

You have an incredibly robust prompt, skill, and standard structure. Pi will happily load your skills directly from `~/.agents/skills/`. However, to fully automate your multi-agent QA pipelines and MCP integrations without manual prompting, you will need to leverage Pi's Extension API to build the orchestration layer that Pi intentionally leaves out.

content.text:
Here is a deeper dive into **#2: Workflow Compatibility (Sub-Agents)** and exactly how your `superpowers.md` workflow clashes with Pi’s philosophy, along with how to solve it.

### The Core Conflict
Your `superpowers.md` Phase 4 mandates strict, isolated role-switching:
1. **Controller** plans the task.
2. **@coder** is dispatched to write the code.
3. **@qa** is dispatched twice (once for spec compliance, once for code quality).

Most "Agent" frameworks try to build this orchestration under the hood. **Pi explicitly refuses to do this.** By default, Pi is a single, continuous conversational stream. If you just ask Pi to "act as the Coder and then act as QA," the LLM will accumulate all those personas in its context window. Eventually, it will start grading its own homework leniently because the "QA" and the "Coder" are sharing the exact same memory stream.

To achieve your Subagent-Driven Development in Pi, you have three distinct paths ranging from manual to fully automated.

---

### Path 1: The Native Pi Way (Session Branching)
Instead of background sub-agents, you leverage Pi's incredibly powerful **session tree** (`/tree`, `/fork`, `/clone`) to isolate contexts manually.

1. **The Controller (Trunk):** You start a standard Pi session. You do Phase 1 (Brainstorming) and Phase 2 (Planning). 
2. **The Coder (Branch):** Once the plan is ready, you type `/clone`. This creates a parallel timeline of the session. You type: `Load ~/.agents/roles/coder.md and execute step 1 of the plan.` Pi writes the code in this isolated branch.
3. **The QA (Branch):** Once the coder finishes, you use `/tree` to jump back to the Trunk (the Controller). You type `/clone` again to create a *new* branch. You type: `Load ~/.agents/roles/qa.md. Look at the git diff and review it against the plan.`
4. **Resolution:** If QA fails, you swap back to the Coder branch, fix the code, and swap back to QA.

**Pros:** Zero coding required. Complete context isolation (the QA branch hasn't seen the Coder's struggles, so it remains objective).
**Cons:** Highly manual. You are the orchestrator moving between branches.

---

### Path 2: The Pi Extension Way (Programmatic Orchestration)
If you want the true "Dispatch" automation described in your `superpowers.md`, you must write a **Pi Extension** (`.ts` file). Because Pi has a programmatic SDK, an extension can spawn background headless instances of Pi.

You would write an extension that registers custom tools or commands like `/dispatch`:

```typescript
// ~/.agents/extensions/subagents.ts
export default function (pi: ExtensionAPI) {
  pi.registerCommand("dispatch", {
    description: "Dispatch a sub-agent (coder, qa) to perform a task",
    action: async (args) => {
      const [role, ...taskParts] = args;
      const task = taskParts.join(" ");
      
      pi.writeMessage(`Spawning background ${role} agent for task: ${task}...`);
      
      // 1. Load the specific role prompt (e.g., ~/.agents/roles/coder.md)
      // 2. Use Pi's SDK to spawn a headless session (RPC or direct SDK)
      // 3. Pass the task and let the background model loop until finished
      // 4. Return the result to the main Pi interface
      
      pi.writeMessage(`✅ ${role} completed the task. Run 'git diff' to review.`);
    }
  });
}
```

**Pros:** Exactly matches your `superpowers.md` workflow. Fully automated.
**Cons:** Requires you to write and maintain the TypeScript extension to handle the background SDK/RPC state management.

---

### Path 3: The UNIX / Tmux Way
Pi’s philosophy heavily leans on standard terminal multiplexing rather than reinventing window management. 

Instead of sub-agents being a feature *inside* Pi, they are just different terminal panes:
* **Pane 1 (Controller):** `pi --system-prompt ~/.agents/roles/controller.md`
* **Pane 2 (Coder):** `pi --system-prompt ~/.agents/roles/coder.md`
* **Pane 3 (QA):** `pi --system-prompt ~/.agents/roles/qa.md`

You discuss the architecture in Pane 1. When the plan is ready, you switch to Pane 2, tell it to read the plan and execute it. When it's done, you switch to Pane 3 and tell it to review the diff.

**Pros:** Extremely UNIX-y. Complete visibility into what every "sub-agent" is doing in real-time. Native support for your existing `~/.agents/roles/` files.
**Cons:** Requires you to manually Alt-Tab / Tmux-switch between panes to pass the baton.

---

### Summary Recommendation
If you switch to Pi, **start with Path 1 (Branching via `/clone`)** to keep things contained in a single terminal without breaking the context isolation your workflow demands. If you find the manual branching tedious, invest an afternoon into building **Path 2 (An Extension)** to create a true `/dispatch` command that runs headless Pi SDK sessions in the background.

content.text:
You are hitting on the exact philosophical tradeoff of the Pi harness. By intentionally removing built-in "Auto-GPT" style autonomous looping and sub-agents, Pi stops the LLM from going down massive, expensive rabbit holes without your consent. 

However, if your goal is **"fire-and-forget" automation for a large project** (where you approve a design doc and walk away while the system writes the whole app), the manual branching or Tmux approaches completely fail.

To achieve true hands-off automation for large projects in Pi, you have to build the automation *around* the agent. Here are the three ways to achieve large-scale automation with Pi, ordered from easiest to most robust:

### 1. The "Single-Agent" Loop (Prompt-Driven Automation)
Pi will continuously execute tool calls in a loop until it decides the user's prompt is fulfilled. You can automate a large project without sub-agents by relying on a strict file-based state machine.

You create a `TODO.md` file with the exact steps. Then you give Pi a prompt like:
> "Read `TODO.md`. For each unfinished step:
> 1. Execute the implementation.
> 2. Run the tests.
> 3. If tests fail, fix the code.
> 4. Once tests pass, check off the item in `TODO.md`.
> Do not stop until all items in `TODO.md` are checked off."

**How it works in Pi:** Pi's tool execution loop will read the file, write code, run bash commands, and update the file, looping autonomously. 
**The Catch:** For a very large project, the context window fills up, and the LLM eventually forgets its early instructions. Pi's auto-compaction helps, but context degradation is inevitable in a single massive session.

### 2. The Extension Approach (The "Delegation Tool")
To prevent context degradation while maintaining automation, you write a Pi Extension that gives the Controller a custom tool: `delegate_task`.

```typescript
// The LLM calls this tool: 
// delegate_task({ role: "coder", instructions: "Build the auth middleware" })
```

When the Controller LLM calls this tool, your TypeScript extension intercepts it. The extension uses the Pi SDK to spin up a **completely fresh, headless Pi session** in the background, injected with the `coder.md` system prompt. It waits for the background session to finish, and returns a summary (e.g., "Files modified: auth.ts. Tests passed.") back to the main Controller.

**How it caters to automation:** The Controller writes the plan, then autonomously loops through the plan by calling the `delegate_task` tool for every step. The Controller's context stays clean (it only sees the summaries), while the background sub-agents do the heavy lifting in isolated contexts. You get full automation without context collapse.

### 3. The SDK Orchestrator (The "Factory" Approach)
If you want absolute, deterministic control over the automation pipeline, you don't use the Pi CLI interface at all. Instead, you write a standalone Node.js script using the `@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent` SDK.

You write a script that implements your `superpowers.md` workflow strictly in code (a state machine):

1. **Script spawns Controller session:** Asks for a JSON plan.
2. **Script parses JSON.**
3. **`for (const task of plan.tasks)` loop begins in Node.js:**
4. **Script spawns Coder session:** Passes the task. Waits for completion.
5. **Script spawns QA session:** Asks it to review the git diff.
6. **If QA fails:** Script passes the QA notes back to the Coder session.
7. **If QA passes:** Script commits the code and moves to the next task in the `for` loop.

### Summary
Pi does not have a "Develop Large Project" button. It is a harness. 

If you want an autonomous swarm of agents building a large project while you sleep, **Approach #3 (The SDK Orchestrator)** is what Pi was built for. Pi provides the engine, the tool integration, the session management, and the LLM routing. You write the 50 lines of TypeScript `for`-loops that strictly define *how* the agents hand off work to one another according to your `superpowers.md` standards.

content.text:
Based on your identity as a Systems Engineer (explicit control, minimal footprint, automation over manual processes, direct/technical), here is the architecture of AI development stripped of marketing fluff. 

Think of LLMs not as "smart assistants," but as stateless functional processors. You input text, they output text. To build software, you must wrap them in an architecture.

Here is the stack, from the bottom up:

### 1. The LLM (The CPU)
Stateless. No memory. No ability to execute code. It only predicts the next token based on the context window you provide. 

### 2. Tools (Syscalls)
APIs exposed to the LLM. You give the LLM a JSON schema saying, "You can call `bash(command)`." The LLM outputs a JSON string requesting that call. *The LLM does not execute the tool; the environment running the LLM does.*

### 3. The Harness (The Runtime / OS)
**This is what Pi is.** A harness is the runtime execution loop that wraps the LLM. 
* **The Loop:** It sends your prompt to the LLM -> parses the LLM's tool request -> executes the tool locally (e.g., runs the bash script) -> appends the raw stdout/stderr to the context -> sends it back to the LLM -> repeats until the LLM says "I'm done."
* **State Management:** It manages the context window (memory). If the context gets too large, the harness handles compaction/truncation so the API doesn't throw a token limit error.
* **Why Pi fits your identity:** Most harnesses (like Cursor or Auto-GPT) are bloated IDEs or massive black-box Python frameworks. Pi is a minimal Node.js REPL. It provides the execution loop and zero magic.

### 4. An Agent (A Daemon / Process)
An Agent is simply a Harness + a System Prompt + a loop constraint. 
When you load `~/.agents/roles/coder.md` into Pi, you instantiate a "Coder Agent." It runs in a continuous loop until the requested feature is complete.

### 5. Context Pollution (Memory Leaks)
If you ask a single Agent to design an app, write the code, and test it, its context window fills up with brainstorming garbage, failed test logs, and out-of-date code. The LLM gets "confused" and its output degrades. In systems terms, the state is polluted.

### 6. Sub-agents (Child Processes)
To solve context pollution, you isolate state. You spin up a fresh Harness instance (a sub-agent) with a blank memory, a hyper-specific system prompt (e.g., `@qa`), and exactly one task. It executes, returns a pass/fail boolean, and terminates.

### 7. The Orchestrator (The Scheduler)
The logic that routes tasks between sub-agents. 
* In your current setup, **you** are the orchestrator. You read the plan, open a terminal, and trigger the Coder.
* To automate this (as discussed previously), you write a script using the Pi SDK. The script acts as `systemd`. It reads your JSON plan, spawns the Coder process, waits for the exit code, and then spawns the QA process.

### 8. MCP - Model Context Protocol (Standardized IPC)
Historically, if you wanted an Agent to read your TickTick tasks or query a database, you had to write custom tool-binding code specific to Pi, Claude Code, or Cursor. 
MCP is a universal protocol (like LSP for language servers). You run an MCP server locally (your `mcp-broker`). Any modern harness can connect to it over stdio or SSE. It standardizes how tools and context are exposed to the Harness.

### Summary for your architecture:
You want explicit control and automation. A monolithic, auto-magic AI tool violates this. 
By using a minimal **Harness** (Pi SDK) as your engine, defining **Agents** as isolated state machines (your `roles/*.md`), and writing an **Orchestrator** script to handle the pipeline, you maintain absolute systemic control over the generation loop.

content.text:
You have hit on the exact boundary between **what an LLM thinks it is doing** and **what the system is actually executing**. 

You are entirely correct: in your `~/.agents` setup, the "Controller/Orchestrator" is just a role (a system prompt) loaded into the LLM. 

However, there is a fundamental difference between the **Orchestrator Persona** (the LLM) and the **Orchestrator Mechanism** (the code that actually isolates memory). Let's clear this up:

### The Problem: An LLM Cannot Actually "Spawn" Anything
If you load `roles/controller.md` into Pi, the LLM will adopt the persona of the Orchestrator. It will write a brilliant plan. It will say, *"I am now dispatching the Coder sub-agent."*

But under the hood, **nothing actually spawned.** 
Because Pi (by default) does not have a `spawn_subagent()` tool, the LLM just continues generating text in the exact same session, using the exact same context window. It is essentially just playing dress-up. It acts like the Coder, then acts like the QA, all within one massive, degrading memory stream. 

### How to make the Orchestrator Role "Real" in Pi
To make your Controller/Orchestrator role actually behave like a multi-agent system, the Harness needs to provide the LLM with the mechanical ability to spin up fresh instances. 

You do this via a **Pi Extension** (which is just a small TypeScript file). 

Here is how the automation actually flows when set up correctly:

1. **The Brain (Instance A):** You start Pi with your `roles/controller.md` prompt. This is your main Orchestrator instance.
2. **The Tool:** You write a Pi Extension that registers a new tool called `dispatch_agent(role: string, task: string)`.
3. **The Action:** The Controller LLM decides it's time for the Coder to work. It outputs a JSON request to call the `dispatch_agent` tool.
4. **The Spawn:** The Pi Harness intercepts that tool call. Your TypeScript extension code runs, and **it** uses the Pi SDK to spawn a completely fresh, invisible background instance of Pi (Instance B) loaded with `roles/coder.md`. 
5. **The Return:** Instance B does the work, finishes, and the extension returns a text summary (e.g., "Code written and pushed") back to Instance A.

### Summary
Yes, the Orchestrator is a role that Pi takes on. But for that role to successfully automate a large project without its memory collapsing, the Harness must provide it with a custom tool (an extension) that programmatically spawns new, isolated Pi instances to do the heavy lifting. 

Without that extension, the Orchestrator role is just a single instance talking to itself, pretending to be a team.

### Flow Walkthrough (Following the Numbers):

**Phase 1: Planning & Delegation**
1. You interact entirely in **Instance A** (the normal Pi terminal).
2. The LLM acts as the **Controller/Orchestrator**. It creates the step-by-step plan.
3. Instead of writing the code itself (polluting its memory), it emits a tool call: `dispatch_agent("coder", "Implement Step 1")`.

**Phase 2: The Spawn (The Automation)**
7. The Pi Execution Loop catches this tool call and hands it to your **Custom Pi Extension**.
8. The Extension code runs. It uses the `createAgentSession` SDK function (like the script I showed you earlier) to spawn **Instance B**.
9. **Instance B** boots up in the background. It is injected with the `coder.md` persona. It has a completely empty, fresh memory state.

**Phase 3: Execution & Review**
10. Instance B loops internally, writing code and modifying your `git worktree`. Once it finishes the task, it exits.
11. The Extension then automatically spawns **Instance C**, injected with the `qa.md` persona, and asks it to review Instance B's changes.
12. If Instance C approves, the Extension collapses all that background activity into a short text summary (e.g., *"Step 1 complete. Tests pass."*).
13. The Extension hands that summary back up to **Instance A**.

**The Result:** 
The Orchestrator (Instance A) reads the summary, checks off Step 1, and emits a new tool call to dispatch the coder for Step 2. The Orchestrator's context window stays clean and small, allowing it to automate a massive project without ever getting confused.

Here is a diagram breaking down the core internal components of the **Pi Harness** itself. It shows how Pi is structured to be a minimal execution engine surrounded by layers of extensibility.

```text
=============================================================================
                          PI HARNESS INTERNAL ARCHITECTURE
=============================================================================

                      [ USER / INTEGRATION LAYER ]
  ┌──────────────┬──────────────────┬──────────────────┬──────────────────┐
  │              │                  │                  │                  │
  │ Interactive  │  Print / JSON    │     RPC Mode     │  TypeScript SDK  │
  │     TUI      │  (CLI pipelines) │ (Process Comm.)  │ (Custom Scripts) │
  │              │                  │                  │                  │
  └──────┬───────┴────────┬─────────┴────────┬─────────┴────────┬─────────┘
         │                │                  │                  │
=============================================================================
                          THE CORE ENGINE (The Loop)
=============================================================================
         │
  ┌──────▼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                         SESSION MANAGER                             │
  │                                                                     │
  │ 1. State Machine: Handles the "Prompt -> Tool Call -> Result" loop  │
  │ 2. Session Tree: Manages branching, forks, and history tracking     │
  │ 3. Context Compactor: Auto-summarizes history to prevent overflow   │
  └──────┬───────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────┬──────┘
         │                           │                           │
  ┌──────▼─────────────┐      ┌──────▼─────────────┐      ┌──────▼─────────────┐
  │ CONTEXT DISCOVERY  │      │   MODEL REGISTRY   │      │   TOOL REGISTRY    │
  │                    │      │                    │      │                    │
  │ Auto-loads files:  │      │ Routes requests to │      │ Safely executes:   │
  │ - SYSTEM.md        │      │ cloud/local APIs:  │      │ - read, write      │
  │ - AGENTS.md        │      │ - Anthropic, OpenAI│      │ - edit (precise)   │
  │ - CLAUDE.md        │      │ - Gemini, Local    │      │ - bash (system)    │
  └──────┬─────────────┘      └──────┬─────────────┘      └──────┬─────────────┘
         │                           │                           │
=============================================================================
                    THE EXTENSIBILITY LAYER (Your Add-ons)
=============================================================================
         │                           │                           │
  ┌──────▼───────────────────────────▼───────────────────────────▼──────┐
  │                                                                     │
  │ 🧩 EXTENSIONS (.ts)   : Arbitrary code. Can add tools, UI widgets,  │
  │                         custom commands, or intercept the engine.   │
  │                                                                     │
  │ 🛠️ SKILLS (SKILL.md) : Markdown instructions teaching the model     │
  │                         how to execute specific workflows.          │
  │                                                                     │
  │ 📝 PROMPTS (.md)      : Reusable snippets injected via /templates.  │
  │                                                                     │
  │ 🎨 THEMES             : UI colors and styling for the terminal.     │
  │                                                                     │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Component Breakdown

1. **User / Integration Layer (The Interface)**
   * **Interactive TUI:** The rich terminal interface where you type, see thinking blocks, and manage the session tree using commands like `/tree` and `/fork`.
   * **Print/JSON:** For standard shell scripting (e.g., `cat file.txt | pi -p "Summarize"`).
   * **RPC:** A strict JSONL protocol allowing non-Node apps (like a Python GUI or an editor plugin) to drive the Pi engine.
   * **SDK:** The raw programmatic building blocks (as seen in the earlier script example).

2. **The Core Engine (The Loop & State)**
   * **Session Manager:** The heart of Pi. It maintains the JSONL file holding the session history. It handles the continuous back-and-forth loop between the LLM and the tools.
   * **Context Compactor:** When the context window fills up, this subsystem silently summarizes older messages to keep the session alive without crashing the model.

3. **The Three Pillars of Execution**
   * **Context Discovery:** Before any LLM request is made, Pi automatically crawls your directories to inject your `AGENTS.md` rules and system prompts.
   * **Model Registry:** Abstracted routing. Whether you use Claude, GPT-4, or a local model, Pi translates the generic "Tool Call" into the specific API format that model requires.
   * **Tool Registry:** The execution environment. It contains the built-in fundamental tools (`bash`, `read`, `edit`, `write`). It guarantees safety (e.g., handling timeouts and truncating massive tool outputs).

4. **The Extensibility Layer (Where you live)**
   * This is where Pi diverges from other harnesses. Instead of hardcoding features like "Sub-agents" or "MCP Brokers" into the Core Engine, Pi expects you to drop a `.ts` file into the `extensions/` folder or a `SKILL.md` into the `skills/` folder to modify the Engine's behavior dynamically.

content.text:
### 1. The Terminology: "AI CLI" vs. "Agent" vs. "Harness"

"AI CLI" is a superficial term. It just describes the user interface. `gh copilot` is an AI CLI, but it’s just a glorified autocomplete. It doesn't actually *do* anything on its own.

From a systems engineering perspective, here is the exact taxonomy:

*   **An Agent:** The conceptual entity (The LLM + a specific System Prompt + a loop). It is the *worker*.
*   **An Agent Framework:** Heavy developer libraries (like LangChain or AutoGen) used to build web apps or complex backend services.
*   **An AI Software Engineer (Devin, Aider, OpenCode, Claude Code):** Highly opinionated, "batteries-included" programs. They have strong opinions on how you should write code, commit to git, and manage files. They act as your autonomous coworker.
*   **A Harness (Pi):** A mechanical term. A harness is a piece of equipment that securely holds a power source (the LLM) and connects it to a load (your OS/filesystem), allowing a human to *steer* it. It provides the runtime, the I/O translation, and the safety constraints—nothing more.

**"Harness" is the most accurate term for Pi.** Pi doesn't want to be your coworker; it wants to be the engine block you build your customized automation upon.

---

### 2. Pi vs. OpenCode / Claude Code / Aider

To understand the difference, think of operating systems: **OpenCode/Claude Code are like macOS or Ubuntu. Pi is like Arch Linux.**

Here is the technical breakdown of how Pi differs from the opinionated ecosystem:

#### A. Opinionated Workflows vs. Absolute Extensibility
*   **OpenCode / Claude Code:** They come with built-in features you can't easily turn off. They have built-in plan modes, built-in sub-agent routing, and built-in MCP (Model Context Protocol) brokers. They dictate how the LLM should think.
*   **Pi:** Pi explicitly refuses to include these. It provides 4 basic tools (`bash`, `read`, `write`, `edit`). If you want an MCP broker, sub-agents, or a "plan mode", you must write or install a TypeScript Extension to inject that logic. It forces you to define the workflow (which perfectly matches your `~/.agents/superpowers.md` setup).

#### B. Conversation History (Linear vs. Tree)
*   **The Others:** Memory is linear. If the agent goes down a bad rabbit hole, you have to hit `Ctrl+C`, undo all its file changes manually, and start a new prompt.
*   **Pi:** Memory is a Git-like tree. Pi saves every step of the conversation as a node. If the agent messes up at Step 5, you can use `/tree`, jump back to Step 4, and `/clone` a new branch of the conversation. You fork the session just like you fork source code.

#### C. Permission Models and Safety
*   **The Others:** Claude Code and others inject interactive (Y/N) prompts when the LLM tries to run a destructive bash command or access certain directories. This is safe, but it breaks "fire-and-forget" automation because it blocks the execution loop waiting for your keyboard input.
*   **Pi:** Assumes you are either running it in a safe environment (like a worktree or container) or that you have configured your own constraints. It will execute the tools. If you want permission gates, you write a Pi Extension to intercept the tool calls and enforce your specific security policy.

#### D. The Editing Mechanism
*   **The Others:** Many tools use "diff patching" (like standard `git diff`) or AST-based (Abstract Syntax Tree) parsing. Diffs often fail if the LLM hallucinates whitespace, and ASTs break on syntax errors.
*   **Pi:** Uses a strict `edit` tool based on exact text replacement (`oldText` -> `newText`). It forces the LLM to target a specific, non-overlapping block of code. It is less "smart" but highly deterministic and less prone to corrupting files.

### Summary

If you want a tool that you can just `npm install`, open, and say "build me a website," and you are okay with how the tool decides to do it—use OpenCode, Aider, or Claude Code.

If you are a Systems Engineer who demands explicit control, who already has a custom `~/.agents/` framework, and who wants to orchestrate isolated state-machines deterministically using an SDK—you use a **Harness** like Pi.

content.text:
In the broader tech industry, no. The industry marketing term is usually **"AI Agent"**, **"Agentic CLI"**, or **"Coding Assistant"**.

However, from a strict systems engineering perspective—and in the official documentation for Pi itself—**yes, the term is "Harness."** 

Here is why that distinction matters:

*   **Industry Term ("AI Agent"):** Implies the software *has agency* or intelligence. It implies a monolithic product that thinks for itself.
*   **Engineering Term ("Harness"):** Derived from "test harness." It is a structural wrapper. A test harness doesn't write tests; it provides the execution environment, mocking, and I/O hooks for the code being tested. 

Pi calls itself a **"terminal coding harness"** because it operates exactly like a test harness. It provides the execution loop, the filesystem hooks (tools), and the terminal UI, but it relies entirely on the external "engine" (the LLM) to drive the logic. 

So if you are talking to a generic developer, you'd call it an "AI CLI agent." If you are discussing system architecture, you call it a "harness."
what makes a harness agentic?
Agentic implies goal-directed autonomous execution

A harness becomes **"agentic"** when you introduce an autonomous, closed-loop execution cycle that is directed by a goal rather than step-by-step human prompts.

If a harness is just a mechanical wrapper (Prompt -> API -> Result), here is the exact threshold where it crosses into being "agentic":

### 1. The Autonomous Feedback Loop (The "ReAct" Loop)
A basic CLI (like standard ChatGPT) stops after one action. You say "write a script", it gives you the code, and it stops.

A harness becomes agentic when it implements a **Reasoning and Acting (ReAct)** loop without human intervention. 
1. **Goal:** "Fix the bug in `auth.ts`."
2. **Action:** The LLM uses the `bash` tool to run the test suite.
3. **Feedback:** The harness captures the failing test output and feeds it directly back to the LLM.
4. **Reasoning:** The LLM reads the failure, realizes its first attempt was wrong, and uses the `edit` tool to change the code.
5. **Action:** It runs the tests again.
6. **Termination:** The loop only breaks when the tests pass (the goal is achieved) or it hits a hard iteration limit. 

### 2. Tool Invocation Rights
A system is not agentic if the human has to execute the tools. The harness must give the LLM the systemic authority to invoke "syscalls" (reading files, executing bash, rewriting code) directly on the host machine. 

### 3. Contextual Self-Correction
An agentic harness must manage state so the LLM can self-correct. If the LLM runs a bash command that throws a `SyntaxError`, the harness doesn't just crash. It traps that stderr, packages it into a system message, and hands it back to the LLM saying, *"Your last tool call failed with this error. Try again."*

### Summary
A simple CLI takes a prompt and returns an answer.
A **Harness** takes a prompt, provides tools, and manages the execution environment.
A Harness becomes **Agentic** when it connects the output of those tools directly back into the LLM's input, creating a self-sustaining loop that hunts for a goal until it succeeds.

content.text:
### 1. Re-grounding in Pi's Philosophy

Pi’s philosophy is **"Aggressive Extensibility over Built-in Magic."** 

It explicitly rejects the bloated "all-in-one" approach of tools like Auto-GPT or Claude Code. Pi dictates:
*   **No built-in Sub-Agents.** (It confuses the context window; build it yourself if you want it).
*   **No built-in MCP (Model Context Protocol).** (Build an extension if you need API integrations).
*   **No built-in Plan Mode or To-Do tracking.** (Write plans to a file).
*   **No permission popups.** (Run it in a safe environment or build your own gatekeeper).

Pi provides the terminal UI, the fundamental tools (`read`, `write`, `edit`, `bash`), the context compactor, and the ReAct execution loop. **Everything else must be an Extension, a Skill, or an SDK script.**

---

### 2. Assessing Your Setup: Path to an Agentic Harness

Because Pi provides the autonomous ReAct loop out of the box, it is *already* agentic at a basic level (it will loop tool calls until a goal is met). However, to make it an Agentic Harness that executes **your specific `.agents` Subagent-Driven Development workflow**, you need to build the "glue." 

Here is exactly what needs to be done to bridge your setup into Pi:

#### Step 1: Flatten Your Context Includes (Easy)
*   **The Issue:** Your `~/.agents/AGENTS.md` uses a custom include syntax (e.g., `@.agents/context/identity.md`). Pi does not auto-expand `@` tags inside markdown files upon load (it uses `@` for terminal UI fuzzy-searching).
*   **The Fix:** You need a simple bash script or Pi Extension that concatenates `identity.md`, `environment.md`, and `long-term.md` into a single `~/.pi/agent/SYSTEM.md` file whenever you boot up.

#### Step 2: Build the MCP Extension (Medium)
*   **The Issue:** Your setup relies heavily on your custom `~/.config/opencode/mcp-broker/` (which holds TypeScript/Python MCPs). Pi does not support MCP natively.
*   **The Fix:** You must write a Pi Extension (`~/.pi/agent/extensions/mcp.ts`). This extension will connect to your local MCP broker, pull the available tools, and map them to Pi’s `pi.registerTool()` API. This gives the Pi LLM access to your specific skills (like TickTick, Obsidian, etc.).

#### Step 3: Build the Sub-Agent Orchestrator Extension (Advanced)
*   **The Issue:** Your `superpowers.md` strictly requires routing tasks between the Controller, Coder, and QA roles. Because Pi refuses to do this natively, running it all in one session will cause context degradation.
*   **The Fix:** You need to write a Pi Extension (`~/.pi/agent/extensions/orchestrator.ts`).
    *   This extension registers a new tool for the main LLM: `dispatch_subagent(role: "coder" | "qa", task: string)`.
    *   When the Controller LLM calls this tool, your extension uses Pi's programmatic SDK (`createAgentSession()`) to spin up a headless, background instance of Pi.
    *   The extension injects the specific role prompt (e.g., `~/.agents/roles/qa.md`), lets the background loop run until completion, and returns the summarized text back to the Controller.

#### Step 4: Adapt Your Skills (Zero Work)
*   **The Good News:** Your current `skills/` directory uses the `SKILL.md` format. Pi natively supports the Agent Skills standard. You simply point Pi to your folder (or let it auto-discover it if it's in the working directory tree), and Pi will immediately understand how to use your existing skills (like `algorithmic-art`, `caveman`, etc.).

### The Verdict
To make your setup work flawlessly in Pi, you don't need to change your underlying philosophy. You just need to spend a day writing **TypeScript Extensions** to implement MCP and Sub-Agent routing. Once those extensions are written, Pi will act as the perfect, minimal engine block for your strict, distributed AI workflows.

content.text:
Yes, absolutely. You have identified the exact reason why Pi’s creator explicitly refused to build native MCP (Model Context Protocol) support into the core engine.

From a systems engineering perspective, **MCP is inherently wasteful with tokens.** Here is why, and why Pi's approach (and your `SKILL.md` setup) is often superior:

### 1. Schema Bloat (The System Prompt Tax)
To use a tool via MCP, the harness must inject the full JSON schema of that tool (parameters, types, descriptions, enums) into the system prompt for *every single API request*. 
* If you have 20 tools on your MCP broker, you are sending thousands of tokens of JSON schema to the LLM on every turn, even if the user just said "Hello." 
* This drains context windows rapidly and increases API costs.

### 2. Payload Bloat (The Return Tax)
MCP servers typically wrap existing REST APIs and return raw, nested JSON objects. LLMs consume a lot of tokens parsing curly braces, null fields, and boilerplate metadata that they don't actually need to solve the problem.

### 3. The "Agent Skills" Alternative (CLI + Markdown)
Pi advocates for the **Agent Skills standard** (which you are already using in `~/.agents/skills/SKILL.md`). This is a vastly more token-efficient architecture.

Instead of registering complex JSON schemas via MCP, you write a standard CLI script (e.g., a Python script that hits your TickTick API and prints plain text). 
Then, you write a `SKILL.md` file that says:
> *"To manage tasks, run `ticktick-cli add "Task"`. Do not use this unless the user asks."*

**Why this is better:**
* **Lazy Loading:** Pi only loads the `SKILL.md` if the LLM decides it needs it, or if you explicitly invoke `/skill:ticktick`. 
* **Zero Schema Overhead:** The LLM just uses its native, highly-optimized `bash` tool to run the command. There are no massive JSON schemas injected into the system prompt.
* **Plain Text Output:** CLI tools usually output grep-able plain text rather than massive JSON payloads, saving return tokens.

### Summary
If you have a massive MCP broker with dozens of tools, it will choke your context window. 

To keep your Pi setup lean and aligned with your "explicit control, minimal footprint" identity: **Avoid bridging your entire MCP broker into Pi if possible.** Instead, wrap your Python/TypeScript MCP logic into simple, standalone CLI executables, and map them using your existing `SKILL.md` architecture. It is faster, cheaper, and exactly how Pi was designed to operate.

---
name: ntv-worktree-manager
description: Orchestrate git worktree creation, synchronization, and setup across the NTV ecosystem (api-v1, dashboard-v1, player-server, player-ui). Use whenever the user starts work on a new ticket, story, or bug.
---

# NTV Worktree Manager

A skill for managing the synchronized git worktrees across the four core NTV repositories, ensuring your development environment is consistent across the entire ecosystem.

## When to use this skill
- Starting a new ticket (`feat`, `fix`, `hotfix`, `test`).
- Setting up a new worktree for a specific task.
- Ensuring repositories are fetched and initialized after creating a worktree.

---

## Workflow: Create/Sync Worktrees for a Ticket

This workflow uses the local environment variables from `~/.bashrc` to dynamically determine paths.

1.  **Ask for Details**: Prompt the user for:
    - **Ticket ID** (e.g., `456`)
    - **Description** (e.g., `fix-auth-bug`)
    - **Type** (e.g., `feat`, `fix`, `hotfix`)
    - **Selected Repos** (Which of `api`, `dash`, `server`, `ui` need the worktree?)
2.  **Construct Branch Name**: Formulate the standard branch: `[type]/[ID]-[Description]` (e.g., `fix/456-fix-auth-bug`).
3.  **Validate Path**: Ensure the `*-worktrees` directory exists for the selected projects.
4.  **Execute**: Invoke `scripts/manage_worktrees.sh` with the ticket details.
5.  **Report**: Summarize the created directories and provide the next step (e.g., `ntv wt [branch-name]`).

---

## Safety Guidelines
- **NEVER** overwrite an existing worktree directory without explicit user confirmation.
- **ALWAYS** perform a `git fetch origin` before creating a new worktree to ensure the base branch is up-to-date.
- **VERIFY** all paths before executing `git worktree add`.
- **USE** the provided `manage_worktrees.sh` script to maintain consistency.

## Bundled Resources
- `scripts/manage_worktrees.sh` (The executor for git operations)
